content.text: Agent instructions are the
complete system-level brief

written into the system prompt to govern an autonomous agent across its entire multi-step run. Where a single-turn instruction tells the model how to answer one question, agent instructions tell it how to behave across an entire autonomous session — covering mission, tools, memory, loop configuration, and termination.
What it is

Agent instructions are the five-pillar specification inside a system prompt that makes a model safe to run autonomously for multiple iterations. The five pillars are: mission (what to accomplish and within what scope), tool manifest (which tools are available and how to use them), memory policy (what state to track across iterations), loop configuration (max iterations and error handling), and stop conditions (observable signals that indicate success, failure, or the need to escalate).

Agent instructions differ from a simple role description in one key way: they must remain coherent across an unpredictable sequence of tool results and partial observations, not just a single user message. Every decision the agent makes during its run is shaped by what these instructions say.
Why it matters

Under-specified agent instructions produce predictable failure modes. Without a clear mission boundary, the agent invents sub-goals and expands scope beyond what was intended. Without explicit stop conditions, it loops indefinitely or stops too early and reports a false completion. Without an error policy, it either retries a failing tool call forever or silently skips the failure and produces an incorrect result. Without tool constraints, it calls tools in an unsafe order or with invalid arguments.

Over-specified instructions produce the opposite problem: a rigid script that cannot adapt when the environment does not match the expected sequence. The balance — clear enough to be safe, flexible enough to recover from surprises — comes from separating intent from procedure. Instructions should say what the agent is trying to achieve and what the hard limits are, not prescribe the exact sequence of tool calls.
Benefits
Well-written agent instructions provide concrete engineering advantages across the full lifecycle of an agent deployment:
Predictable multi-step behavior
— the same instructions produce consistent behavior across runs on similar inputs, making evaluation and regression testing tractable.
Enforced safety constraints
— constraints in the system prompt are applied on every iteration of the loop, not just the first step. A rule stated once governs every tool call the agent makes.
Auditable decision trail
— when the agent's instructions specify what it should track in its scratchpad, every run produces a readable log of what the agent saw and decided.
Easier debugging

— when something goes wrong, clear instructions make it obvious whether the failure was an instruction gap (the agent was never told what to do) or a model limitation (it was told but did not follow).
Safe multi-agent composition

— in a multi-agent system, each sub-agent with well-specified instructions can be reasoned about independently. The orchestrator's job is simpler when it can trust each sub-agent to operate within its stated scope.
Maintainability
— instructions written as typed components with a builder function can be version-controlled, diffed, and tested like application code.
Trade-offs
Every decision in agent instruction design involves a trade-off. Understanding these tensions helps you calibrate instruction depth for a given deployment:
Under-specification vs. over-specification
— too little guidance leaves the model free to drift; too much turns it into a rigid script that cannot recover from unexpected tool outputs. Aim for intent, not procedure.
Concreteness vs. adaptability
— concrete constraints (name specific tools, enumerate exact stop signals) improve safety but reduce the agent's ability to handle edge cases the instructions did not anticipate.
Instruction length vs. context budget

— every token in the system prompt appears in every API call. A 1000-token instruction set on a 20-step run consumes 20 000 extra input tokens. Keep instructions concise and move task-specific details to the initial user turn.
Completeness vs. contradiction risk
— adding more constraints increases the chance of contradictions. Contradictory instructions cause unpredictable behavior because the model tries to satisfy both simultaneously.
Stability vs. per-run customization
— stable system prompts are cached by the API, reducing cost. Prompts that change per run are not cached. If you need per-run context, append it as a clearly labelled dynamic section at the end.
How it works

The system prompt is passed unchanged to every API call throughout the agent run. Each iteration of the loop — think, act, observe, decide — is shaped by the instructions written there. Here is how each pillar governs a specific phase of the loop:
The Five Pillars and Their Loop Roles
Pillar
Governs
Failure if missing
Mission
THINK — frames what the agent reasons about
Agent invents sub-goals, expands scope
Tools & Permissions
ACT — constrains which tool calls are legal
Agent calls unauthorized or mis-ordered tools
Memory Policy
OBSERVE — specifies what to record from each result
Agent loses track of state across iterations
Loop Config
DECIDE — sets iteration budget and error handling
Infinite loops or unhandled failures
Stop Conditions
DECIDE — provides observable exit signals
Agent loops forever or stops prematurely

The instructions do not control the agent's internal reasoning — they shape its frame of reference. A mission statement is not a script; it is the context within which all reasoning happens. Similarly, stop conditions are not code that the runtime checks; they are natural-language signals the model learns to recognize and act on.
Architecture

The diagram below shows how the five pillars of agent instructions map to the agent loop phases. Dashed lines indicate which instruction component governs each phase. The loop continues until a stop condition is detected or max_iterations is reached.

attrs.alt: Agent instructions governing the autonomous loop — five pillars mapped to loop phases

content.text: When to use it
Agent instructions in their full five-pillar form are appropriate whenever:
The agent runs more than one LLM call autonomously without user input between calls.
The agent has access to write or mutate operations (file writes, database inserts, API calls that create side effects). Read-only agents can tolerate lighter specifications.
Correctness of the agent's decisions must be auditable after the fact — for compliance, debugging, or user trust.
The agent is part of a multi-agent pipeline and must behave predictably within a defined scope so the orchestrator can trust its outputs.
The task has multiple possible outcomes (success, failure, escalation) and the agent must route to the correct one.
The agent runs in production and its behavior needs to be governed by an explicit policy that can be reviewed, approved, and version-controlled.
Lighter specifications are appropriate for:
Single-turn assistants with no autonomous tool calls.
Prototypes where you are exploring what the agent should do, not yet enforcing what it must not do.
Internal dev tools where the risk of scope expansion is low and auditability is not required.
How to implement
Building Agent Instructions with a Dataclass

The most maintainable pattern is to define agent instructions as a typed Python dataclass with a to_system_prompt() method. This separates the content of instructions from their formatting, makes each pillar a named field with type checking, and lets you instantiate different agents from the same class:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class AgentInstructions:
    """Typed container for all five pillars of agent instructions.

    Serialise to a system prompt string via to_system_prompt().
    The result is stable and suitable for Anthropic API prompt caching.
    """

    mission: str
    """One to three sentences: what the agent must accomplish and its scope."""

    tools: list[str]
    """Names of tools the agent is permitted to call, in usage-priority order."""

    tool_notes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    """Optional constraints on tool usage (authentication requirements, ordering rules)."""

    memory_policy: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    """What the agent must track across iterations (e.g. 'Record every file modified')."""

    max_iterations: int = 20
    """Hard upper bound on loop iterations. The agent must stop if it reaches this."""

    error_policy: Optional[str] = None
    """How to handle tool failures: retry count, fallback action, escalation path."""

    stop_conditions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    """Concrete signals that indicate the task is complete or should be escalated."""

    output_format: Optional[str] = None
    """How the agent should structure its final answer and per-step trace."""

    def to_system_prompt(self) -> str:
        """Return a formatted system prompt string from these instructions."""
        parts: list[str] = [self.mission.strip()]

        if self.tools:
            tool_lines = "
".join(f"- {t}" for t in self.tools)
            notes = ""
            if self.tool_notes:
                notes = "
" + "
".join(f"  Note: {n}" for n in self.tool_notes)
            parts.append(f"Tools you may call:\n{tool_lines}{notes}")

        if self.memory_policy:
            lines = "
".join(f"- {m}" for m in self.memory_policy)
            parts.append(f"Memory policy:\n{lines}")

        loop_lines = [f"- Maximum iterations: {self.max_iterations}"]
        if self.error_policy:
            loop_lines.append(f"- Error policy: {self.error_policy}")
        parts.append("Loop configuration:\n" + "
".join(loop_lines))

        if self.stop_conditions:
            lines = "
".join(f"- {s}" for s in self.stop_conditions)
            parts.append(f"Stop when:\n{lines}")

        if self.output_format:
            parts.append(f"Output format:\n{self.output_format.strip()}")

        return "

".join(parts)
Mission is a string, not a list
— the mission statement is prose — it should read as a coherent job description, not a list of requirements. Lists are for tools and stop conditions.
Tools are enumerated by name
— listing tool names explicitly prevents the agent from hallucinating tool names it was never given.
max_iterations is a number, not prose

— saying 'maximum 8 iterations' in the instructions is less enforceable than having a separate runtime counter. Both belong in the design: the instruction sets the agent's expectation; the runtime enforces it as a hard limit.
to_system_prompt returns stable output
— given the same inputs, the method always returns the same string. This is the precondition for effective prompt caching.
Testing Agent Instructions

Because agent instructions are the primary configuration for an autonomous system, they warrant unit tests. Test that the builder produces the correct string, that required sections are present, and that contradictory states are impossible:

import pytest
from agent_instructions import AgentInstructions


class TestAgentInstructions:
    def test_mission_appears_first(self):
        inst = AgentInstructions(
            mission="You are a billing agent. Process refunds.",
            tools=["lookup_invoice"],
        )
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        assert prompt.startswith("You are a billing agent.")

    def test_all_tools_listed(self):
        tools = ["lookup_invoice", "issue_refund", "send_email"]
        inst = AgentInstructions(mission="Process refunds.", tools=tools)
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        for tool in tools:
            assert tool in prompt

    def test_max_iterations_present(self):
        inst = AgentInstructions(mission="Process refunds.", tools=[], max_iterations=5)
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        assert "5" in prompt

    def test_stop_conditions_present(self):
        inst = AgentInstructions(
            mission="Process refunds.",
            tools=[],
            stop_conditions=["refund confirmed", "escalation created"],
        )
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        assert "refund confirmed" in prompt
        assert "escalation created" in prompt

    def test_empty_optional_sections_omitted(self):
        inst = AgentInstructions(mission="Process refunds.", tools=[])
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        # memory_policy and output_format should not appear when empty
        assert "Memory policy" not in prompt
        assert "Output format" not in prompt

    def test_error_policy_present_when_set(self):
        inst = AgentInstructions(
            mission="Process refunds.",
            tools=[],
            error_policy="Retry once, then escalate.",
        )
        prompt = inst.to_system_prompt()
        assert "Retry once" in prompt
Examples
A Complete Billing Support Agent

The following example uses the AgentInstructions dataclass to instantiate a billing support agent, then runs it against a single ticket using the Anthropic SDK. The agent loop continues until the model produces an end_turn stop reason (task complete) or max_iterations is reached:

import anthropic
import json
from agent_instructions import AgentInstructions  # from code block 1

# Define the agent's complete instructions once.
BILLING_AGENT = AgentInstructions(
    mission=(
        "You are a billing support agent for a SaaS product. "
        "Your goal is to resolve billing tickets end-to-end: look up the invoice, "
        "determine the correct action, and either process a refund or escalate. "
        "Scope: one ticket per run. Do not modify unrelated accounts."
    ),
    tools=["lookup_invoice", "issue_refund", "create_escalation", "send_email"],
    tool_notes=[
        "Call lookup_invoice before any other tool — you must have invoice data first.",
        "issue_refund requires that the invoice status is 'paid'; check before calling.",
        "create_escalation takes a reason string; keep it under 200 characters.",
    ],
    memory_policy=[
        "Record the invoice ID and status after lookup_invoice.",
        "Record the action taken (refund or escalation) and its outcome.",
        "If a tool fails, record the error message and the retry count.",
    ],
    max_iterations=8,
    error_policy=(
        "On tool error, retry once with identical arguments. "
        "If the retry also fails, call create_escalation with the error message."
    ),
    stop_conditions=[
        "issue_refund returns success.",
        "create_escalation returns a ticket ID.",
        "The user confirms the issue is resolved.",
        "max_iterations reached — call create_escalation with reason='max iterations'.",
    ],
    output_format=(
        "After each tool call, write one sentence: what you did and what you found.\n"
        "When done, write: OUTCOME: <one of REFUND_PROCESSED, ESCALATED, RESOLVED>.\n"
        "Then write: INVOICE_ID: <id> and ACTION: <brief description>."
    ),
)

# The system prompt is stable across every call in the agent loop.
SYSTEM = BILLING_AGENT.to_system_prompt()

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_billing_agent(ticket_description: str) -> str:
    """Run the billing agent for one support ticket."""
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": ticket_description}]

    for step in range(BILLING_AGENT.max_iterations):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=2048,
            system=SYSTEM,
            tools=[
                {"name": "lookup_invoice", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"invoice_id": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["invoice_id"]}},
                {"name": "issue_refund", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"invoice_id": {"type": "string"}, "amount": {"type": "number"}}, "required": ["invoice_id", "amount"]}},
                {"name": "create_escalation", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"reason": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["reason"]}},
                {"name": "send_email", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"to": {"type": "string"}, "body": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["to", "body"]}},
            ],
            messages=messages,
        )

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            # Agent has completed the task.
            return response.content[0].text

        # Process tool calls and append results to messages.
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
        tool_results = []
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == "tool_use":
                result = dispatch_tool(block.name, block.input)
                tool_results.append({"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": json.dumps(result)})
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    return "ERROR: max_iterations reached without resolution."


def dispatch_tool(name: str, inputs: dict) -> dict:
    """Route tool calls to their implementations."""
    # Placeholder — real implementations call internal APIs.
    return {"status": "ok", "data": {}}
Key points in this implementation:
SYSTEM is computed once before the loop
— the system prompt does not change between iterations, which keeps the stable prefix eligible for API caching.
Tool results are appended to messages
— each step adds the assistant's tool calls and the corresponding tool results to the messages list. The system prompt remains separate from this list.
stop_reason == 'end_turn' signals completion
— when the model determines the task is done, it stops calling tools and produces a final text response. The loop exits at that point.
dispatch_tool is a routing layer
— in production this function calls real internal APIs. In tests it returns mocked responses, letting you unit-test the agent loop without side effects.
Real-world example

Consider a code review agent that operates in a CI pipeline. The agent is invoked after a pull request is opened and must review the changed files, run the test suite, and post a structured review comment. Its agent instructions look like this:

Mission:
You are a code review agent for a Python web service. Your job is to review the
changed files in the pull request described in the task, run the test suite, and
post a structured review comment. Scope: review only files changed in the PR diff.
Do not modify source files; do not commit or push.

Tools you may call:
- get_pr_diff: returns the unified diff for the PR
- read_file: reads a file from the repository
- run_tests: executes the test suite and returns the result
- post_review_comment: posts a structured comment to the PR

  Note: Call get_pr_diff first — you need the diff before reading any files.
  Note: Call run_tests after reading the changed files, not before.
  Note: Call post_review_comment exactly once, as the final action.

Memory policy:
- Record the list of changed files after get_pr_diff.
- Record the test result (pass/fail + failure count) after run_tests.
- Record each issue found during review (file, line, severity, description).

Loop configuration:
- Maximum iterations: 12
- Error policy: if run_tests fails with a tool error (not a test failure), retry
  once. If the retry also fails, include a note in the review comment that
  test results could not be obtained.

Stop when:
- post_review_comment returns success.
- Maximum iterations reached — post whatever review is available.

Output format:
After each tool call, write one sentence describing what you found.
The review comment must use this structure:
  VERDICT: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | COMMENT
  SUMMARY: <one paragraph>
  ISSUES: <bulleted list, or "None">
  TEST_RESULT: PASS | FAIL | UNAVAILABLE

This example illustrates several real-world patterns: the tool ordering rules prevent the agent from reading files before knowing which files changed; the error policy for run_tests distinguishes between a tool failure (retry) and a test failure (report it); the stop condition ensures the agent always produces output even if it hits the iteration limit; and the output format specifies machine-parseable fields so the CI pipeline can extract VERDICT without parsing prose.
Common mistakes
Most agent instruction failures fall into five patterns. The following annotated code illustrates each one alongside its correct alternative:

# PITFALL 1 — Mission too vague to constrain scope
# Without a clear mission boundary, the agent invents sub-tasks.
BAD_MISSION = "You are a helpful billing agent."
# The model decides what "helpful" means. It may modify unrelated accounts,
# proactively send emails, or invent refund policies.

GOOD_MISSION = (
    "You are a billing support agent for a SaaS product. "
    "Your job is to process the single refund request described in the task. "
    "Do not modify any invoice that is not explicitly mentioned in the task."
)


# PITFALL 2 — Listing tools without constraints
# Listing tool names without notes lets the model call them in any order.
BAD_TOOLS = ["lookup_invoice", "issue_refund", "send_email"]
# The model may call issue_refund before lookup_invoice, producing an error.

GOOD_TOOL_NOTES = [
    "Always call lookup_invoice first — you need invoice data before any action.",
    "Only call issue_refund if invoice status is 'paid'.",
    "Only call send_email after a refund or escalation is confirmed.",
]


# PITFALL 3 — No stop conditions → infinite loop
# Without explicit stop conditions, the model continues generating tool calls
# after the task is done, wasting tokens and causing side effects.
BAD_NO_STOP = """
You are a billing agent. Help the user with their billing issue.
"""
# Missing: when to stop. The agent will keep calling tools indefinitely.

GOOD_STOP_CONDITIONS = [
    "issue_refund returns {'status': 'ok'}.",
    "create_escalation returns a ticket ID.",
    "max_iterations (8) reached.",
]


# PITFALL 4 — Secrets in the system prompt
# System prompts appear in logs, traces, and prompt caching.
# Never embed API keys, tokens, or passwords.
BAD_SECRET = f"Use API key {API_KEY} to call the billing service."
# The key is now in every log entry and cached prompt.

GOOD_SECRET_PRACTICE = (
    "The billing service client is pre-authenticated. "
    "Call lookup_invoice directly — credentials are injected at the tool level."
)
# Tools read secrets from environment variables at call time, not from the prompt.


# PITFALL 5 — Over-specifying the exact sequence of tool calls
# Rigid step-by-step instructions prevent the agent from recovering when
# an earlier step fails or returns unexpected data.
BAD_SEQUENCE = """
Step 1: Call lookup_invoice with the invoice_id.
Step 2: Call issue_refund with the amount from step 1.
Step 3: Call send_email to notify the customer.
"""
# If step 1 fails, the agent has no guidance on what to do next.

GOOD_INTENT = """
Look up the invoice, then decide whether to issue a refund or escalate.
Notify the customer after any action you take.
On any tool error, retry once and then escalate if the retry fails.
"""
# Intent-based instructions let the agent adapt to unexpected outcomes.
Vague mission
— a mission statement that does not bound scope allows the agent to expand into adjacent tasks. Always name what the agent must not do alongside what it must do.
Toolless tool lists
— listing tool names without ordering constraints lets the agent call them in an unsafe sequence. Add at least one ordering note for every tool with a dependency.
Missing stop conditions
— without explicit stop signals, the agent continues generating tool calls after the task is logically complete. Name at least two stop conditions: the success case and the escalation case.
Secrets in the prompt
— system prompts appear in logs and cached responses. Tool implementations should read credentials from environment variables at call time.
Scripted tool sequences
— specifying exact step-by-step tool call order prevents the agent from recovering when an earlier step fails. Use intent-based language instead.
Best practices
Write the mission in one to three sentences
— longer missions dilute focus. If you need more than three sentences, split the agent into two agents with simpler missions.
Enumerate tools by name
— do not say 'use available tools'. Name each tool the agent may call. Tools not named are effectively prohibited.
State prerequisites explicitly
— if tool B requires data from tool A, say so. Do not assume the model will infer the dependency.
Name stop conditions as observable events
— instead of 'stop when done', write 'stop when issue_refund returns {"status": "ok"}'. Observable signals can be tested in evaluation cases.
Set max_iterations to a number you have reasoned about
— count the maximum number of tool calls a successful run could realistically require, then add a small buffer. Do not use 100 as a default.
Include both the success stop condition and the escalation stop condition
— the agent must know what to do when the task cannot be completed, not only when it can.
Keep the error policy in one sentence per case
— error policy prose: 'retry once; if retry fails, escalate.' Ambiguity in error policy leads to silent data loss.
Version agent instructions with the code
— commit instruction changes to version control alongside the code changes they accompany. A diff that changes agent behavior without a visible instruction change is hard to debug.
Test instructions with eval cases
— for each stop condition, write an eval case that verifies the agent reaches that stop condition on appropriate inputs.
Performance & scalability

Agent instructions have a direct impact on per-run cost and latency. Because the system prompt is included in every API call, its token count multiplies with the number of iterations. On a 20-step run, a 500-token system prompt costs 10 000 extra input tokens; a 2000-token prompt costs 40 000. The Anthropic API caches stable system prompts, so those tokens are charged at the cache read rate (significantly cheaper than the standard input rate) on subsequent calls — but only if the prompt is stable. Any change to the prompt breaks the cache and charges the full input rate for that call.
Keep instructions under 900 tokens
— shorter prompts cache more reliably and leave more context window for tool results and conversation history.
Place stable content before dynamic content
— if you inject per-run state into the system prompt, append it after the stable sections. The cache matches on prefix.
Remove examples from the system prompt
— examples that illustrate how to use a tool belong in the initial user turn, not the system prompt. They are task-specific, not always-applicable, and they inflate the prompt unnecessarily.
Calibrate max_iterations empirically

— run a sample of real tasks and measure the 95th-percentile iteration count. Set max_iterations to that value plus a 20% buffer. Overshooting wastes tokens; undershooting causes premature termination.
Tooling & ecosystem
Agent instruction patterns are supported across major LLM agent frameworks, though the terminology and structure vary:
Anthropic SDK (Python/TypeScript)
— pass instructions as the system= parameter to client.messages.create. The SDK supports prompt caching via cache_control blocks on stable system prompt sections.
LangChain AgentExecutor
— agent instructions map to the system_message and agent_kwargs parameters. The agent's tool list is passed separately but should align with what the instructions name.
LlamaIndex agent runners
— OpenAIAgent and ReActAgent accept a system_prompt or prefix_messages parameter that plays the same role as agent instructions.
OpenAI Assistants API

— instructions are stored as part of the Assistant object and are applied to every thread run. The five-pillar structure applies but the deployment model is different — instructions are server-side, not sent per-call.
Semantic Kernel (C#/Python)
— the KernelArguments and PromptTemplateConfig objects serve a similar purpose. The mission maps to the description field; constraints map to the plugin's metadata.

Regardless of framework, the five pillars remain the same design target. The framework determines where and how the instructions are stored and passed; the content principles described here apply uniformly.
Comparison with alternatives
Agent instructions (natural-language specifications in a system prompt) are one of several approaches to governing autonomous behavior. Each has different expressiveness and safety properties:
Approach
Expressiveness
Safety
Testability
Best for
Agent instructions (this topic)
High — natural language, adaptable
Depends on instruction quality
Eval cases on stop conditions
LLM-based agents with multi-step reasoning
Hard-coded workflow (DAG)
Low — only predefined paths
High — no model discretion
Unit tests on each node
Deterministic pipelines with no branching logic
State machine
Medium — predefined states and transitions
High — transitions are explicit
State coverage tests
Processes with a small number of known states
Behavior tree
Medium — priority-ordered subtrees
Medium — structure enforces priority
Subtree unit tests
Robotics and game AI; less common for LLM agents
Code-only agent (no LLM)
Low — only what you code
Very high
Standard unit tests
Tasks where every path can be enumerated in advance

The key advantage of agent instructions over hard-coded workflows is that the agent can handle inputs the author did not anticipate. The key risk is that the model may not follow the instructions precisely. Evaluation cases that exercise each stop condition and constraint are the primary mitigation.
Security & reliability

Agent instructions are a security boundary. Because they govern what tools the agent may call and under what conditions, gaps in the instructions are potential attack surfaces in both directions: the agent doing too much (scope expansion) and the agent doing too little (silent failure).
Never embed secrets in instructions

— system prompts appear in API logs, tracing systems, and prompt caches. API keys, database passwords, and tokens must be passed via environment variables and read by tool implementations at call time, never baked into the prompt.
Scope restrictions are security constraints

— the 'do not modify unrelated accounts' or 'do not modify files outside /workspace/myapp' clauses are not quality guidance — they are security boundaries. Write them with the same care as application-layer authorization checks.
Require confirmation before irreversible actions

— for tools that make irreversible changes (deleting records, sending emails, processing payments), specify in the instructions that the agent must confirm with the user before proceeding if there is any ambiguity in the task.
Log what the agent did
— the memory policy should include logging each tool call and its outcome to an audit trail. This is not just helpful for debugging — it is a reliability requirement for production systems.
Implement a hard iteration counter in the runtime

— the max_iterations instruction sets the agent's expectation, but the agent loop in your code must also enforce this as a hard stop. Both are necessary: the instruction shapes the model's behavior; the runtime is the backstop.
Validate tool outputs before acting on them

— if the agent uses tool output to construct subsequent tool calls, validate the output schema before using it. A malformed tool result that the agent acts on without checking can cascade into incorrect behavior.

Prompt injection is a real risk for agents with broad tool access. If any tool returns content from an external source (web pages, user-submitted data, file contents), that content could contain instructions designed to override the agent's system prompt. Mitigations include: quoting tool results as data (not instructions), constraining the agent's scope to a minimal set of tools, and running the agent with the least privilege necessary for the task.
Key takeaways
Agent instructions are the five-pillar system prompt specification that makes an LLM safe to run autonomously: mission, tools, memory policy, loop configuration, and stop conditions.

Unlike single-turn instructions, agent instructions must remain coherent across an unpredictable sequence of tool results and partial observations — write them for the whole run, not just the first step.
Mission statements bound scope: name not just what the agent must do but what it must not do.
Tool manifests enumerate tools by name and include prerequisite ordering notes.
Stop conditions must be observable events, not vague completion states. Name both the success condition and the escalation path.
max_iterations is a number you reason about, not an arbitrarily large default.
Never embed secrets in the system prompt. Pass credentials via environment variables read at tool-call time.
Build instructions as typed components with a builder function; version them with the code.
Test instructions with eval cases that exercise each stop condition independently.
Stable instructions are cached by the API. Append dynamic per-run state as a clearly labelled final section to preserve cache hit rate.
Mental model

Think of agent instructions as a job brief given to a new contractor on their first day: it tells them what the job is, what resources they have access to, what the ground rules are, how to handle problems, and how to know when the job is done. A good brief does not script every action — it gives the contractor enough context to make good decisions on their own. A poor brief either leaves gaps that the contractor fills arbitrarily or micromanages every step so rigidly that the contractor cannot adapt when reality diverges from the plan.

The same principle applies to agent instructions. Write the brief, not the script. Every instruction that names a concrete observable outcome or a specific tool name adds safety. Every instruction that specifies the exact sequence of tool calls reduces adaptability. The right balance is: concrete constraints on what must and must not happen, flexible latitude on how to achieve the goal within those constraints.
